coredata.dc:title: Facile strategy for preparing the composite of MoS2 microspheres and N/S dual-doped graphene stabilized by graphene quantum dots for all-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ziqing
Wang, Xixin
Xu, Maodan
Yin, Zekun
Zhao, Jianling
coredata.dc:description: The composite (GQDs-MoS2/NSG) of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and N/S dual-doped graphene (NSG) has been successfully synthesized through in situ self-assembly by a new hydrothermal strategy using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as auxiliary agent. The effect of GQDs dosage on the morphology of MoS2 has been investigated. Results show that adding GQDs to the nanohybrids can significantly improve the dispersion uniformity and binding state of MoS2 and NSG, and increase the overall defect density. When the GQDs dosage is 20 mg, the sample GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 has the best performance, and its specific capacitance reaches 564.3 .Fg−1 at a scan rate of 10 .mV s−1. Under a large current density of 20 .A g−1, the specific capacitance retention rate of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is 92.8% after 10,000 constant current charge/discharge loops. The contribution of the pseudocapacitance of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 at 10–200 mV.s−1 was studied by using dynamic analysis and density functional theory calculation. All-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was fabricated with NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. When the power density is 900 W.kg−1, the energy density reaches 68.8 Wh.kg−1. At a current density of 20 A.g−1, the specific capacitance retention rate is 92.2% after 10000 constant current charge/discharge loops.

head.ce:title: Facile strategy for preparing the composite of MoS2 microspheres and N/S dual-doped graphene stabilized by graphene quantum dots for all-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of materials science and engineering, Hebei university of technology, Tianjin 300130, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The composite (GQDs-MoS2/NSG) of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and N/S dual-doped graphene (NSG) has been successfully synthesized through in situ self-assembly by a new hydrothermal strategy using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as auxiliary agent. The effect of GQDs dosage on the morphology of MoS2 has been investigated. Results show that adding GQDs to the nanohybrids can significantly improve the dispersion uniformity and binding state of MoS2 and NSG, and increase the overall defect density. When the GQDs dosage is 20 mg, the sample GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 has the best performance, and its specific capacitance reaches 564.3 .Fg−1 at a scan rate of 10 .mV s−1. Under a large current density of 20 .A g−1, the specific capacitance retention rate of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is 92.8% after 10,000 constant current charge/discharge loops. The contribution of the pseudocapacitance of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 at 10–200 mV.s−1 was studied by using dynamic analysis and density functional theory calculation. All-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was fabricated with NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. When the power density is 900 W.kg−1, the energy density reaches 68.8 Wh.kg−1. At a current density of 20 A.g−1, the specific capacitance retention rate is 92.2% after 10000 constant current charge/discharge loops.

ce:section.ce:para: Among many electrochemical energy storage devices, supercapacitors have attracted extensive attention because of their advantages combing both traditional capacitors (high power density, long service life) and batteries (high energy density) [1–4]. Supercapacitors can be used in many fields, such as flexible electronics, electric vehicles, military and aerospace, and power grid balance [5–12].

Graphene is a two-dimensional material made of sp2 hybrid carbon. As an allotrope of carbon, graphene has unique properties such as high electron mobility, good mechanical strength, and large specific surface area, thus it has been a research hotspot of electrode materials for supercapacitors [13–18]. However, since graphene has no band gap, its conductivity cannot be completely controlled, moreover, the surface of graphene is smooth and inert, and π-π interactions between the sheets can lead to graphene agglomeration. Existing studies have shown that the introduction of elements such as boron, nitrogen, sulfur and phosphorus into graphene can destroy its uniformity, generate a large number of defects in its crystal structure. This can improve its electrical properties and chemical reactivity, and enhance the energy conversion and storage performance [19–24]. For example, Zeng's research group [25] prepared nitrogen-doped porous graphene (N-rGO). When the current density is 1 A.g−1, the specific capacitance of N-rGO is 390 F.g−1, which is 70% higher than that of rGO (207 F.g−1).

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is similar to graphene in structure. It is a two-dimensional (2D) layered nanostructure composed of S-Mo-S layers under the action of van der Waals forces. It is a new type of active material for supercapacitors [26–31]. Because MoS2 has a wide and weak van der Waals gap, it is easy to insert foreign ions (Na+, K+, H+) between the layers for charge storage [32–35]. However, the poor conductivity of MoS2 is not conducive to the migration of ions into its interior structure, and the agglomeration between layers also hinders the charge storage [36–38]. Due to the characteristics of graphene and MoS2, the composite of the two can realize complementary advantages. On the one hand, graphene can be used as a substrate to fix MoS2, enrich active sites and improve the conductivity and ion transport rate of MoS2. On the other hand, graphene and MoS2 can also inhibit the agglomeration of each other and thus produce synergistic effect. Therefore, the preparation of MoS2/graphene composite is an effective strategy to improve the overall electrochemical performance of the material [39]. See our previous work for details on this part [40].

The morphology and dispersion uniformity of component have a great influence on the properties of the composite [41]. Due to different chemical bond polarity between graphene and MoS2, there is a large interfacial tension between them, which will hinder their uniform dispersion and the improvement of composite properties. In the process of preparing the composite, some auxiliaries can be added to reduce the interfacial tension, improve the dispersion uniformity, and thus enhance its performance. The auxiliaries commonly used are polyvinylpyrrolidone [42], polypyrrolidone[43], N-methylpyrrolidone [44], cetyltriethylammonium bromide[45], sodium decadesylbenzene sulfonate [46], benzyl purpurin [47], and silicotungstic acid [48], etc. However, these auxiliaries have almost no electrochemical activity, and their addition have some negative effects. Therefore, exploring new auxiliaries is an important way to improve the properties of graphene and MoS2 composite.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are new zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, which not only have the characteristics of graphene, but also possess quantum confining effect, edge effect, good dispersion and stability [49–54]. However, using GQDs as an auxiliary agent to synthesize the composite of MoS2 and graphene has not been reported till now. In this work, a novel strategy was used to prepare the composite of MoS2 microspheres and N,S dual-doped graphene (NSG) by one-step hydrothermal method with GQDs as the auxiliary agent and GO, sodium molybdate and thiourea as raw materials. The preparation process is simple and controllable, while the structure of the obtained composite (GQDs-MoS2/NSG) is uniform, and there is a significant synergistic enhancement effect between GQDs-MoS2 and NSG. The asymmetric all-solid supercapacitor (ASC) was assembled with GQDs-MoS2/NSG as the negative electrode and NSG as the positive electrode, and the energy density reaches 68.2 Wh.kg−1 when the power density is 900 W.kg−1.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method [55]. 1 g citric acid and 0.2 g urea were added into 100 mL deionized water and the obtained solution was stirred for 10 min and then reacted at 160 °C for 4 h. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation and dialysed in a 3500 MWCO dialysis bag for 12 h to obtain the GQDs solution. After being dried at 60 °C, solid GQDs was obtained, which was used to prepare 1 mg mL−1 GQDs solution with deionized water.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared using the modified Hummers method[56]. 0.1 g GO was added to 100 mL deionized water to prepare GO dispersion. 0.1 g sodium molybdate dihydrate was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water, and X mL (X = 10, 20, 30) of GQDs solution prepared in Section 2.1 was added. After being stirred for 0.5 h, the GQDs solution was dropped into GO dispersion, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h and stirring for 1 h. Then, 3.2 g thiourea was slowly added, and the mixed solution was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and reacted for 24 h at 200 ℃. The product was repeatedly washed with deionized water and then freeze-dried, and the resulting complex was labeled as GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30).
NSG, GQDS-MoS2 and MoS2/NSG samples were prepared by the same experimental process, and the specific raw material dosage was given in Table 1.

The composition and morphology of the samples were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 450 FEG) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fei Talos F200S) equipped with X-ray energy spectrum. The chemical states of the surface elements of the samples were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250 Xi, Al Kα). X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8-advance, using Cu Kα) and Raman reflex (532 nm) were used to analyze the microstructure of the samples. The specific surface area of the samples was measured by specific surface area tester (ASAP2020M+C). The particle size distribution of the samples was measured by laser nano particle size tester (PSS 380). The viscosity of gel electrolyte was measured by rotary viscometer (NDJ-1). The electrical conductivity of the samples was measured with a dual electrical four-probe tester (RTS-9).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured using a three-electrode system through an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). The electrolyte was 6 M KOH, and the electrode needs to be balanced for 24 h before the test. Detailed measurement and calculation methods can be referred to our previous work [21]. Asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was prepared by using GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 as negative electrode, NSG as positive electrode and KOH/PVA gel as electrolyte.

The specific capacitance was calculated by the following equation (Eq. (1)):
(1) Cs = (I·∆t) / (m.∆V)

where Cs is the specific capacitance (F g−1), I reflects the discharge current (A), ∆t represents the discharge time (s), m is loading mass of the active materials on the electrodes (g), and ∆V represents voltage drop during discharging process (V).

The energy density (E, Wh·kg−1) and average power density (P, W kg−1) of supercapacitors were calculated by Eqs. (2) and (3):
(2) E = (0·5·Cs·∆V2) / 3·6
(3) P = (E·3600) / ∆t
where ∆V is the operating voltage range, and ∆t represents the discharge time (s).

Fig. 1 shows SEM images of sample NSG, GQDs-MoS2, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30). Fig. 1a shows that the NSG is a layered porous structure composed of uniform folds. As can be seen from Fig. 1b, GQDs-MoS2 is composed of microspheres. From the enlarged image of a single MoS2 microsphere, it can be seen that the surface of the microsphere is obviously wrinkled, and it is composed of ultra-thin nanosheets with a thickness of tens of nanometers, presenting a hierarchical structure. When GQDs is not added, there are many blocky MoS2 and flaky NSG aggregated in MoS2/NSG (Fig. 1c). When the amount of GQDs solution is 10 mL, spherical MoS2 appear in GQDs-MoS2/NSG10, with uneven size and partial connection (Fig. 1d). When the dosage of GQDs solution is 20 mL, the size of MoS2 microspheres (about 200 nm in diameter) and dispersion of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 are relatively uniform, and MoS2 microspheres combine closely with NSG, without obvious agglomeration (Fig. 1e). When the amount of GQDs solution is 30 mL, the MoS2 in GQDs-MoS2/NSG30 is still in a spherical structure with uniform size, but its diameter is decresed (about 120 nm), and local stacking and cracking appear (Fig. 1f).

In order to further investigate the microstructure and composition of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20, transmission electron microscopy was used to characterize it. Fig. 2a shows the unique pleated edge structure of the sample GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. The MoS2 microspheres with a diameter of about 200 nm are evenly distributed. The GQDs on the surface of the MoS2 microspheres not only effectively control the size of the microspheres and inhibit their agglomeration, but also act as the anchor points of NSG, making the microspheres be evenly dispersed. The spacing of 0.63 nm and 0.28 nm in Fig. 2b is consistent with the d-spacing values of (002) and (100) crystal faces of MoS2. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 2c clearly shows the polycrystalline structure of MoS2, and the four diffraction rings correspond to the (002), (100), (103), and (110) crystal faces of MoS2 respectively. Fig. 2d-i give the EDS spectrum diagram of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. As can be seen from the image, element C is evenly distributed in the whole composite structure (Fig. 2e), element Mo is concentrated in the nanosphere region (Fig. 2f), element S has a significantly higher concentration in the microspheres region (Fig. 2g), element N has an obvious circular contour distribution (Fig. 2h), and element O has a very small amount in the microspheres region (Fig. 2i). Fig. 2d-i show that the microspheres is mainly composed of Mo and S, the N and S elements are doped into the graphene, and that the combination of graphene and the microspheres is uniform and tight. This is consistent with the results in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2a-c.

Results in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 show that the addition of GQDs into the composite can significantly improve the dispersion uniformity and binding state of MoS2 and NSG, and reduce the diameter of MoS2. This is due to the fact that GQDs have the similar structure to graphene, and they also have good dispersibility and edge effect, which can combine with both NSG and MoS2 well. Therefore, GQDs can reduce the interfacial tension between MoS2 and NSG and thus disperse them more effectively.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of GO, NSG, GQDs-MoS2, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30). As can be seen from Fig. 3a, GO has a strong peak at 2θ= 9.7°, corresponding to the graphene layer spacing d= 0.91 nm. After the hydrothermal reaction, the (001) diffraction peak of GO disappears, and the (002) diffraction peak of NSG appears at 2θ= 24.7° with d= 0.36 nm, indicating that GO is reduced and the lattice structure of graphene is restored to a certain extent. The diffraction peaks of GQDs-MoS2 at 14.1°, 33.5°, 39.4° and 58.6° correspond to the (002), (100), (103) and (110) crystal faces of MoS2 (JCPDS No.01–37–1492). The patterns of MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are similar to those of GQDs-MoS2 (Fig. 3b), both of which only have the diffraction peak of MoS2, without the diffraction peak of NSG at 2θ= 24.7°, indicating that the recombination of MoS2 effectively prevents the agglomeration of graphene. According to the (002) diffraction peak of MoS2, the grain sizes of GQDs-MoS2, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) were calculated to be 8.79, 9.21, 8.72, 6.58 and 5.81 nm, respectively. As the amount of GQDs increases, the grain size of MoS2 in the sample decreases, indicating that the presence of GQDs inhibits the growth of MoS2 crystal plane. In addition, with the addition of GQDs, the diffraction peak of MoS2 is shifted to a small angle, indicating that the intercalation between MoS2 and doped graphene is further enhanced, and GQDs inhibits the growth of MoS2 grains.

Fig. 4a shows the Raman spectra of NSG, GQDs-MoS2, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30). G peak at 1582 cm−1 represents the complete sp2 lamellar structure of the material. D peak at 1379 cm−1 is induced by the defects and disorder of the lamellar structure of the material. I

of NSG, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are 1.08, 1.13, 1.25, 1.41 and 1.32, respectively. According to the following equations (Eqs. 4 and 5) [57]:
(4)

their corresponding defect densities are 3.15 × 1011 cm−2, 3.45 × 1011 cm−2, 4.23 × 1011 cm−2, 5.38 × 1011 cm−2 and 4.71 × 1011 cm−2, respectively. The GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 has the highest defect density. The two peaks near 386 and 428 cm−1 are attributed to the E2 g vibration mode of the in-plane displacement of Mo–S and the A1 g vibration mode of the out-of-plane symmetric displacement, respectively. The E2 g and A1 g peaks of GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are weakened when GQDs is added. Fig. 4b is the Raman contour map of 100 points of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. There is a high-brightness continuous line around 1380 and 1580 cm−1 respectively, corresponding to the intensity of peak D and G at different positions. Fig. 4c shows the contour plot of I
D
/I
G
in Raman image. Within an area of 17.5 × 17.5 µm2, I
D
/I
G
of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is concentrated in the range of 1.3–1.5, and the corresponding defect densities are from 4.57 × 1011 cm−2 to 6.09 × 1011 cm−2. Based on histogram gauss function fitting (Fig. 4d), the average value (μ) of I
D
/I
G
is 1.405, and the average value of relative standard deviation (σ) is 0.952. Fig. 4 shows that the introduction of MoS2 reduces the size of the sheets in NSG and increases the defect density of NSG. GQDs can effectively inhibit the agglomeration of MoS2, and the dosage of GQDs has a significant effect on the disorder degree and defect density of composite, which is consistent with Fig. 1 and Fig. 3.

In order to further study the chemical composition and valence states of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20, XPS measurement was conducted. As can be seen from Fig. 5a, the C in GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 has five chemical states, which are C–S–C bond at 284 eV, C=C bond at 284.5 eV, C–O bond at 284.9 eV, C–N bond at 285.9 eV, and C=O bond at 288.4 eV[21]. After peak fitting of the N1 s peak of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 (Fig. 5b), it can be seen that the C atom is substituted with the N atom in graphene in the form of pyridinic N (399.4 eV), pyrrolic N (400.8 eV) and graphitic N (401.4 eV). Fig. 5c shows the S2p high-resolution spectrum of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. The peaks at 160.6 and 161.7 eV correspond to S2p2/3 and S2p1/2 of S2- in MoS2, respectively, and the small peaks at 167.8 eV belong to S–C doping in NSG. During the hydrothermal process, thiourea releases ammonia, sulfur ions and cyanamide, which can reduce the functional groups and replace some carbon atoms in graphene in the form of graphite nitrogen, pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen, thiophene sulfur and sulfur oxides. Fig. 5d shows the MO3d high-resolution spectrum of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. The peaks at 229.9 and 233.2 eV correspond to Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2 of Mo4+ in MoS2, respectively, and the weak peaks at 226.9 eV correspond to S2 s. XPS analyses show that the sample is a composite of MoS2 and N/S dual-doped graphene, in which the contents of Mo, S, C, N and O are 27.64, 19.86, 36.35, 7.61, 8.54 wt%, respectively. Fig. 5e is the structural schematic diagram of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. Under the action of GQDs, the MoS2 microspheres are uniformly anchored on the NSG porous network, which ensures the stability of microstructure and the rapid electron transfer. The MoS2 microspheres form many channels between graphene sheets, which is conducive to the penetration of electrolyte into the material and to the diffusion and migration of the ions.

The specific surface area and particle size distribution of each sample are shown in Fig. 6. The specific surface areas of NSG, GQDs-MoS2, MoS2/NSG and GQDs- MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are 273.8, 37.4, 215.5, 287.6, 304.5, 265.3 m2 g−1 (Fig. 6a). Results show that the microsphere structure of MoS2 is formed after the addition of GQDs as an additive, and the specific surface area is significantly improved. In addition, MoS2 content also affects the overall specific surface area. According to Fig. 6b, the median particle size D50 of GQDs-MoS2, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are 242, 415, 328, 225 and 119 nm, respectively. According to Eq. (6), the particle size distribution widths of the four activated carbons were calculated. The SPAN values of particle size dispersion of GQDs-MoS2, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are 1.9, 3.7, 2.5, 1.6 and 1.7, respectively. The results show that the distribution uniformity of MoS2/NSG is the worst, and the distribution uniformity of GQDs-MoS2, GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 and GQDs-MoS2/NSG30 is similar, among which the particle uniformity of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is the best, which is consistent with the results obtained by SEM observation.
(6) SPAN = (D90﹣D10) / D50

As can be seen from Table 2, the conductivity of NSG is significantly higher than that of GQDs-MoS2. With the addition of GQDs, the conductivity of the composite (GQDs-MoS2/NSG) is improved. With the increase of GQDs, the diameter of MoS2 microspheres decreases and the number of MoS2 microspheres increases, and the conductivity of GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) decreases. The conductivity of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is lower than that of NSG, but the capacitance performance is higher than that of NSG, mainly because MoS2 can provide pseudocapacitance, and the unique microsphere structure has a high specific surface area, which is conducive to charge storage.

Fig. 7a-f show the CV curves of GQDs-MoS2, NSG, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30). When the potential is lower than − 0.8 V, the CV curves of GQDs-MoS2 bend downward drastically, indicating that the redox reaction (MoS2 + K+ + e- ⇌ MoS-SK) is weak, which is due to the larger internal resistance (Fig. 7a). The CV curves of NSG are approximately rectangular and have the characteristics of an ideal double-layer capacitance (Fig. 7b). The CV curves of MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are similar to those of NSG, and have a very weak redox peak (Fig. 7c-f), indicating that the composite of MoS2 and NSG significantly improves the electrical conductivity of MoS2. According to Fig. 7g, at the same scan rate, the capacitance value increases in the order of GQDs-MoS2, NSG, MoS2/NSG, GQDs-MoS2/NSG10, GQDs-MoS2/NSG30 and GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. When the scan rates are 10 mV s−1 and 200 mV s−1, the capacitances of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 are 564.3 F g−1 and 488.2 F g−1, respectively, and the capacitance retention rate is 86.5%. In addition, the capacitance of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is significantly higher than the sum of GQDs-MoS2 and NSG, indicating that there is a synergistic effect between MoS2 and NSG.

According to the above analyses, the addition of GQDs to the composite can weaken the agglomeration of MoS2 and NSG, and significantly improve the dispersion uniformity and binding state of MoS2 and NSG. In addition, the disorder degree and defect density of the composite are increased, and thus the composite has more active sites and higher utilization rate. Therefore, the capacitance performance of the composite is improved. However, the addition of excessive GQDs will result in smaller MoS2 microspheres in agglomerate state, which leads to the decrease of the overall capacitance value.

Fig. 7h shows the GCD curves of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 at different current densities, which are approximately triangular, indicating that the electrode reaction has a very good reversibility and approximates the double-layer capacitance. According to Fig. 7i, the specific capacitance retention rate of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is 92.8% after 10000 constant current charge/discharge loops at a current density as large as 20 A g−1. The GCD curves only change slightly under different cycles (the insert in Fig. 7i), indicating that the sample has good cycle stability.

As can be seen from Fig. 8 and Fig. 1e, after 10000 loops, the morphology of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 does not change significantly (Fig. 8a), the EIS curve shifts slightly to the right, and the resistance increases slightly (Fig. 8b), which is consistent with Fig. 7i.

Based on the excellent performance of the GQDs-MoS2/NSG20, the charge storage mechanism of the material was investigated through dynamic analyses. According to the existing literature [58], the response current value (I) of CV curve is related to the scanning rate (ν) as shown in Eq. 7, where the value of β can reflect the storage mechanism of the material. When β = 0.5, it is diffusion-controlled, which is the battery property. When β = 1, it is surface-controlled, which is the capacitance property. The log(I)-log(ν) curve of the GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 under the potential of − 0.3 V, − 0.6 V and − 0.9 V can be obtained from Fig. 7e and Eq. 7 and is given in Fig. 9a. The β values obtained by linear fitting are 0.88, 0.84 and 0.82, respectively, which are all between 0.5 and 1, indicating that the electrode exhibits the combined properties of battery and capacitor. The contribution of the battery and capacitance can be quantitatively calculated by Eq. 8, where k1ν is the surface control current (capacitance) and k2ν1/2 is the diffusion control current (battery).
(7) i = ανβ
(8) i(V) = k1ν + k2ν1/2

Fig. 9b gives the distribution of each control process in the CV curve of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 at 10 mV·s−1. According to Fig. 7e and Eq. (8), the capacitance distribution of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 at different scan rates can be shown in Fig. 9c. As can be seen from Fig. 9b and c, when the scan rate increases from 10 to 200 mV s−1, the contribution of the surface capacitance of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 increases from 67.6% to 78.3%.

In order to explore the reason for the increase of the surface capacitance ratio, according to the redox reaction (MoS2 + K+ + e- ⇌ MoS-SK), based on the first principles calculation, we used density functional theory (DFT) method to simulate the transfer of K+ in the GQDs-MoS2/NSG20, and compared the stored energy barrier of K+ in two ways. As shown in Fig. 9d, for the convenience of calculation, single-layer and double-layer MoS2 models are taken from the constructed multi-layer MoS2 for analysis. After lattice relaxation, the lattice parameters of the two models are a = b = 8.264 Å and a = b = 8.271 Å, respectively. In the monolayer MoS2 model, K+ diffuses on the surface. In the two-layer MoS2 model, K+ diffuses between layers, and the diffusion path is from the top of Mo to S. Through calculation, the energy barriers of K+ transfer on the single MoS2 layer surface and the double MoS2 layer are 1.15 eV and 1.67 eV, respectively, indicating that the transfer of K+on the surface of MoS2 layer is easier than between layers. Therefore, the contribution of the surface capacitance increases with the scan rate.

Fig. 10a shows the EIS spectra of GQDs-MoS2, NSG, MoS2/NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30). The insert figure shows the high-frequency region and equivalent circuit of the EIS spectra. In the equivalent circuit, Rs is the internal resistance, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, and Zw represents Warburg diffusion resistance. Admittance coefficient (Y0) is an important parameter reflecting Zw. The larger the value of Y0 is, the stronger the diffusion ability of the material and the better the high magnification performance are. Considering the microstructure of the electrode, the capacitance of the electrode was fitted by using CPE. The resulting values of Rs, Rct and Y0 of GQDs-MoS2, NSG, MoS2/NSG, and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are shown in Table 3. It can be seen from Table 3 that the electrode resistances (Rs, Rct and Zw) of GQDs-MoS2 are large, and Rs, Rct and Y0 of GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are all small. According to Raman spectrum analysis in Fig. 4, GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 has the highest defect density, which shortens the charge migration path in the electrode material and weakens the electrochemical polarization in the electrochemical reaction. Therefore, Rs, Rct and Zw of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 are all the lowest. Fig. 10b shows the Bode diagram of GQDs-MoS2, NSG, MoS2/NSG, and GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30). For an ideal supercapacitor, the phase angle in the low-frequency region should be close to 90° in the phase-frequency diagram, while the phase angles in the low-frequency region of GQDs-MoS2, NSG, MoS2/NSG, GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) of the measured samples are 55°, 68°, 74°, 81°, 86° and 77°, respectively. When the phase angle is 45°, the characteristic frequencies f
0 are 0.09, 0.13, 0.22, 2.23, 5.54 and 0.67 Hz, respectively, and the corresponding response time constants τ (τ = 1/f
0 ) are 11.1, 7.69, 4.55, 0.45, 0.18 and 1.49 s, respectively. The τ values of GQDs-MoS2/NSGX (X = 10, 20, 30) are much lower than that of most traditional activated carbon electrochemical capacitors (τ
0 ≈10 s), indicating the excellent rate performance. Fig. 10c is the amplitude-frequency diagram of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20, in which lg Z represents the impedance amplitude. In the amplitude-frequency diagram, the impedance amplitude in the high frequency region tends to be 0. When the frequency is lower than 100 Hz, the amplitude shows an increasing trend with a slope of about 1, indicating that the GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 has a high stability, and its operating frequency range is expanded, meaning that it can still work normally at higher frequencies. Fig. 10d shows the relation between the capacitance (C) and the frequency of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20. At high frequency region, the capacitance value is close to 0, indicating the GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is close to the pure resistance element in this case. As the frequency decreases, the capacitance value tends to increase. When the frequency is lower than 100 Hz (diffusion threshold), the capacitance value tends to be stable, and is about 2.9 F. Under this circumstances, the capacitance value is independent of the frequency, and the GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 is equivalent to a pure capacitance. Diffusion threshold can be used to define the resistance and capacitance characteristics of supercapacitor. The smaller the diffusion threshold is, the greater the power loss will be. Otherwise, the capacitive characteristics are better, which can make the capacitor carry out high-power input and output. Since GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 can provide a more smooth transmission channel for the electrolyte ions, its surface can carry out faster ion transport and adsorption, which improves the diffusion threshold and expands the working frequency range of the supercapacitor.

In order to verify the practicability of the best sample GQDs-MoS2/NSG20, NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 were used as positive and negative electrodes respectively, and their dosage was matched by the principle of charge balance. KOH/PVA gel was used as electrolyte, and the water content of the gel was 56.5%. An all-solid asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was simply assembled and its electrochemical performance was tested. Fig. 11a shows the schematic diagram of the assembled ASC device. Fig. 11b shows the CV curves of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 and NSG electrodes at 10 mV s−1, and their stable potential windows are − 1.2–0 V and 0–0.6 V, respectively. The redox peaks of NSG may be caused by nitrogen, sulfur and residual oxygen-containing functional groups. Fig. 11c shows the CV curves of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20//NSG under the potential range of 0–1.8 V at different scanning rates. The CV curves are well shaped at 200 mV s−1, proving that the device has low resistance and high rate performance. As shown in Fig. 11d, the approximately symmetrical charging-discharge curve reflects the high Coulomb efficiency and excellent electrochemical reversibility of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20//NSG. Fig. 11e shows the EIS spectrum of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20//NSG in the range of 0.01 Hz-100 kHz. Straight-line with large slope at the low frequency region and circular arc with small slope at the high frequency region indicate that it has ideal capacitance behavior. The phase angle of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20//NSG at the low frequency region is 85°. When the phase angle is 45°, the f
0 is 0.8 Hz, and the corresponding τ
0 is 1.25 s (the insert in Fig. 11e). The τ value of GQDS-MoS2/NSG20//NSG is much lower than that of the most traditional activated-carbon electrochemical capacitors (τ
0 ≈10 s), indicating that GQDs-MoS2/NSG20//NSG has excellent rate performance. An energy density as high as 68.8 Wh kg−1 can be achieved at the power density of 900 W kg−1, moreover, its energy density can maintain at 54.5 Wh kg−1 under a powder density as high as 18,000 W kg−1. This is a highly competitive result compared with those reported in previous references ( Table 4). Fig. 11f shows the specific power density (P) and energy density (W) of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20//NSG compared with some MoS2 based materials previously reported. As shown in Fig. 11g, the capacitance retention rate of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20//NSG is 92.2% after 10000 constant current charge/discharge loops at 20 A g−1. After 10000 loops, neither GCD curve nor EIS curve changes significantly (Fig. 11g illustration), and the viscosity of KOH/PVA gel changes from 4.17 Pa s to 4.32 Pa s, indicating that the slight performance deterioration of ASC device may be ascribed to the status changes in both electrode materials and the gel electrolyte. Even so, the overall stability of ASC devices is good. Fig. 11h visually shows the picture of the red light-emitting diode (LED, 1.5 V) lit by the assembled ASC device. The actual voltage tested by a digital multimeter is 1.74 V. shows that GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 has a good potential application prospect in the field of energy storage.

In this paper, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used as the auxiliary agent to prepare the composite of MoS2 microspheres and N/S dual-doped graphene (GQDs-MoS2/NSG20) by one-step hydrothermal method. GQDs have similar structure with graphene, and also have a good dispersion and edge effect, so they can combine with both NSG and MoS2 well. Therefore, GQDs can promote the generation of MoS2 microspheres, significantly improve the dispersion state and structural uniformity of MoS2 and NSG, increase the defect density of the composite, and produce synergistic effect between pompous MoS2 and NSG. The optimized sample (GQDs-MoS2/NSG20) has a defect density of 5.34 × 1011 cm−2, a specific capacitance of 564.3 F g−1 (10 mV s−1), and a capacitance retention of 92.8% after 10000 constant current charge/discharge loops (20 A g−1). In addition, the diffusion threshold of GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 reaches 100 Hz, indicating that the working range has been greatly expanded. An all-solid asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was assembled using NSG and GQDs-MoS2/NSG20 as positive and negative electrodes and KOH/PVA gel as electrolyte. When the power densities are 900 W kg−1 and 18,000 W kg−1, the energy densities are 68.8 Wh kg−1 and 54.5 Wh kg−1, respectively, and the specific capacitance retention rate is 92.2% after 10,000 loops (20 A g−1).
